commentary_block: Sirach 24 is Wisdom’s self-praise hymn: she moves from cosmic scope and pre-creation origin, to commanded rest in Jacob/Zion, to explicit identification with the Mosaic Torah in v.23 and the river imagery that follows. Box & Oesterley emphasize the heavenly-court setting in vv.1–2, the tabernacle/Zion localization in vv.10–12, and the cultic fragrance imagery of vv.15–16. Skehan & Di Lella likewise treat the chapter as the book’s great Wisdom/Torah synthesis, with Zion as Wisdom’s chosen dwelling and the rivers of vv.25–27 as overflowing instruction. Verses 18 and 24 are textual additions in some witnesses: NRSVA supplies them by footnote, NETS brackets them, and Charles notes them in apparatus-style material.

sources.charles_apot: R. H. Charles (ed.), The Apocrypha and Pseudepigrapha of the Old Testament in English, vol. 1 (Oxford, 1913), Sirach / Box & Oesterley, Internet Archive djvu text.
sources.nets: A New English Translation of the Septuagint, Sirach (Benjamin G. Wright III), PDF at ccat.sas.upenn.edu.
sources.nrsv: Bible Gateway NRSVA Sirach 24 page, with verses 18 and 24 supplied from footnotes where omitted in the main text.
sources.commentary: Chapter note paraphrases synthesize Oesterley 1913 (public domain) plus brief fact-level paraphrase of Skehan & Di Lella 1987 on Wisdom’s Zion/Torah focus.

1.charles_apot: Wisdom praiseth herself, and is honoured among her people.
1.nets: Wisdom will praise her soul, and in the midst of her people she will boast.
1.nrsv: Wisdom praises herself and tells of her glory in the midst of her people.
1.verse_commentary: Oesterley reads vv.1–2 as Wisdom speaking before the heavenly court, so the public self-praise is a claim about her status both in Israel and among the divine hosts.

2.charles_apot: She openeth her mouth in the assembly of the Most High, and is honoured in the presence of His hosts.
2.nets: In an assembly of the Most High she will open her mouth, and before his power she will boast.
2.nrsv: In the assembly of the Most High she opens her mouth, and in the presence of his hosts she tells of her glory:
2.verse_commentary: The “assembly of the Most High” is treated by Oesterley as the heavenly host, not merely an earthly congregation.

3.charles_apot: I came forth from the mouth of the Most High, and as a mist I covered the earth.
3.nets: I came forth from the mouth of the Most High, and like a mist I covered earth.
3.nrsv: ‘I came forth from the mouth of the Most High, and covered the earth like a mist.
3.verse_commentary: Oesterley links Wisdom’s first-person speech to Proverbs 8 and notes the mist/cloud imagery already hints at a Spirit/Wisdom connection in creation language.

4.charles_apot: In the high places did I fix my abode, and my throne was in the pillar of cloud.
4.nets: I encamped in the heights, and my throne was in a pillar of cloud.
4.nrsv: I dwelt in the highest heavens, and my throne was in a pillar of cloud.
4.verse_commentary: The cloud-pillar imagery pushes the poem toward tabernacle-presence and later Shekinah motifs.

5.charles_apot: Alone I compassed the circuit of heaven, and in the depth of the abyss I walked.
5.nets: A circle of sky I encircled alone, and in the deep of abysses I walked.
5.nrsv: Alone I compassed the vault of heaven and traversed the depths of the abyss.
5.verse_commentary: Oesterley hears cosmological sweep here: heaven’s circuit above and the abyss below.

6.charles_apot: Over the waves of the sea, and over all the earth, and over every people and nation I held sway.
6.nets: In the waves of the sea and in all the earth and in every people and nation I led.
6.nrsv: Over waves of the sea, over all the earth, and over every people and nation I have held sway.
6.verse_commentary: This verse keeps Wisdom universal before the poem narrows to Israel as her resting-place.

7.charles_apot: With all these I sought a resting-place, and (said): In whose inheritance shall I lodge?
7.nets: With all these I sought repose, and in whose inheritance I would settle.
7.nrsv: Among all these I sought a resting-place; in whose territory should I abide?
7.verse_commentary: Skehan & Di Lella treat v.7 as the hinge between Wisdom’s cosmic range and her eventual localization in Jacob/Zion.

8.charles_apot: Then the Creator of all things gave me commandment, and He that created me fixed my dwelling-place (for me); and He said: Let thy dwelling-place be in Jacob, and in Israel take up thine inheritance.
8.nets: The creator of all commanded me: “Encamp in Iakob, and in Israel let your inheritance be.”
8.nrsv: ‘Then the Creator of all things gave me a command, and my Creator chose the place for my tent. He said, “Make your dwelling in Jacob, and in Israel…
8.verse_commentary: The command to dwell in Jacob is the poem’s decisive move from cosmic Wisdom to covenantal, Israel-centered Wisdom.

9.charles_apot: He created me from the beginning, before the world; the memorial of me shall never cease.
9.nets: Before the age, from the beginning, he created me, and until the age I will never fail.
9.nrsv: Before the ages, in the beginning, he created me, and for all the ages I shall not cease to be.
9.verse_commentary: The pre-creation language is important, but in Sirach it serves Wisdom’s durability and authority rather than later metaphysical speculation.

10.charles_apot: In the holy tabernacle I ministered before Him; moreover in Zion was I established.
10.nets: In a holy tent I ministered before him, and thus in Sion I was firmly set.
10.nrsv: In the holy tent I ministered before him, and so I was established in Zion.
10.verse_commentary: Temple/tabernacle service is central: Wisdom ministers before God and is established in Zion.

11.charles_apot: In the Holy City likewise He caused me to rest, and in Jerusalem was my authority.
11.nets: In a beloved city as well he put me down, and in Ierousalem was my authority.
11.nrsv: Thus in the beloved city he gave me a resting-place, and in Jerusalem was my domain.
11.verse_commentary: Skehan & Di Lella emphasize Zion/Jerusalem as Wisdom’s chosen resting-place, not a generic symbol.

12.charles_apot: And I took root among an honoured people, in the portion of the Lord (and) of His inheritance.
12.nets: I took root among a glorified people; in the portion of the Lord is my inheritance.
12.nrsv: I took root in an honoured people, in the portion of the Lord, his heritage.
12.verse_commentary: The rooting image turns Wisdom from heavenly voice into planted covenant presence among the Lord’s inheritance.

13.charles_apot: I was exalted like a cedar in Libanus, and like an olive-tree on the mountains of Zion.
13.nets: Like a cedar I was raised up in Lebanon, and like a cypress in the mountains of Haërmon.
13.nrsv: ‘I grew tall like a cedar in Lebanon, and like a cypress on the heights of Hermon.
13.verse_commentary: The exalted tree imagery marks grandeur, visibility, and sacred plantedness.

14.charles_apot: I was exalted like a palm-tree on the sea-shore, and as rose-plants in Jericho; and as a fair olive-tree in the plain; yea, I was exalted as a plane-tree by the waters.
14.nets: Like a palm I was raised up in Aiggada, and like rosebushes in Iericho.
14.nrsv: I grew tall like a palm tree in En-gedi, and like rose-bushes in Jericho; like a fair olive tree in the field, and like a plane tree beside water I…
14.verse_commentary: En-gedi/Jericho/field/waters imagery broadens the fertility motif; commentators read the flora as abundance, beauty, and cultivated blessing.

15.charles_apot: As cinnamon and aspalathus have I given a scent of perfumes, and as choice myrrh I spread abroad a pleasant odour; as galbanum, and onyx, and stacte; (I was) as the smoke of incense in the Tabernacle.
15.nets: Like cinnamon and camel’s thorn for spices, and like choice myrrh I gave forth a fragrance.
15.nrsv: Like cassia and camel’s thorn I gave forth perfume, and like choice myrrh I spread my fragrance, like galbanum, onycha, and stacte, and like the…
15.verse_commentary: The aromatics and incense imagery evokes Exodus 30 and cultic holiness; Wisdom is bound to acceptable worship.

16.charles_apot: I as a terebinth stretched forth my branches, and my branches were branches of glory and grace.
16.nets: I, like a terebinth, spread out my branches, and my branches were branches of glory and grace.
16.nrsv: Like a terebinth I spread out my branches, and my branches are glorious and graceful.
16.verse_commentary: The terebinth image emphasizes outward spread and visible magnificence.

17.charles_apot: I as a vine put forth grace, and my flowers are the fruit of glory and wealth.
17.nets: I, like a vine, budded forth favor, and my blossoms were the fruit of glory and wealth.
17.nrsv: Like the vine I bud forth delights, and my blossoms become glorious and abundant fruit.
17.verse_commentary: Fruitfulness is both aesthetic and beneficent here—glory and wealth flow outward from Wisdom.

18.charles_apot: Critical addition noted by Charles: “I am the mother of beautiful love, of fear, of knowledge, and of holy hope …”
18.nets: “I am a mother of love that is beautiful, and of reverence … and of knowledge and of devout hope.”
18.nrsv: I am the mother of beautiful love, of fear, of knowledge, and of holy hope; being eternal, I am given to all my children, to those who are named by…
18.verse_commentary: Verse 18 is a secondary/additional line in several witnesses; use it as a textual-variant data point, not as unquestioned base text.

19.charles_apot: Come unto me, ye that desire me, and be ye filled with my produce.
19.nets: Come to me, you who desire me, and from my produce be filled.
19.nrsv: ‘Come to me, you who desire me, and eat your fill of my fruits.
19.verse_commentary: The invitation shifts from self-description to direct summons, much like Wisdom’s invitations in Proverbs.

20.charles_apot: For my memorial is sweeter than honey, and the possession of me than the honey-comb.
20.nets: For the memory of me is sweet beyond honey, and the inheritance of me beyond a honeycomb of honey.
20.nrsv: For the memory of me is sweeter than honey, and the possession of me sweeter than the honeycomb.
20.verse_commentary: Honey imagery marks desirability and delight, not merely intellectual correctness.

21.charles_apot: They that eat shall still hunger for me, and they that drink me shall still thirst for me.
21.nets: Those who eat me will hunger for more, and those who drink me will thirst for more.
21.nrsv: Those who eat of me will hunger for more, and those who drink of me will thirst for more.
21.verse_commentary: The paradoxical hunger/thirst-after-eating underscores inexhaustibility rather than deficiency.

22.charles_apot: He that obeyeth me will not be ashamed, and they that serve me will not commit sin.
22.nets: He who obeys me will not be ashamed, and those who work with me will not sin.
22.nrsv: Whoever obeys me will not be put to shame, and those who work with me will not sin.’
22.verse_commentary: The obedience language closes Wisdom’s speech with moral effect, not only intellectual admiration.

23.charles_apot: All these things are the book of the covenant of God Most High, the Law which Moses commanded (as) an heritage for the assemblies of Jacob.
23.nets: All these things are the book of the covenant of the Most High God, a law that Moyses commanded us.
23.nrsv: All this is the book of the covenant of the Most High God, the law that Moses commanded us as an inheritance for the congregations of Jacob.
23.verse_commentary: This is the chapter’s famous identification of Wisdom with Torah; Skehan & Di Lella treat it as the poem’s theological center.

24.charles_apot: Critical addition noted by Charles: “Faint not, (but) be strong in the Lord … beside Him there is no Saviour.”
24.nets: “Do not cease to be strong in the Lord … there is no savior beside him.”
24.nrsv: ‘Do not cease to be strong in the Lord, cling to him so that he may strengthen you; the Lord Almighty alone is God, and besides him there is no…
24.verse_commentary: Verse 24 is an added line in some witnesses; it matters mainly as a textual/versification issue and as an exhortational gloss tying Torah-wisdom to covenant loyalty.

25.charles_apot: Which filleth (men) with wisdom, like Pison, and like Tigris in the days of new (fruits);
25.nets: It fills wisdom like Phison and like Tigris in days of new things.
25.nrsv: It overflows, like the Pishon, with wisdom, and like the Tigris at the time of the first fruits.
25.verse_commentary: The river images begin to portray Torah/Wisdom as life-giving abundance on an Edenic scale.

26.charles_apot: Which overfloweth like Euphrates, with understanding, and as Jordan in the days of harvest;
26.nets: It supplies understanding like Euphrates and like Jordan in days of harvest.
26.nrsv: It runs over, like the Euphrates, with understanding, and like the Jordan at harvest time.
26.verse_commentary: Jordan-at-harvest language intensifies flood/overflow rather than placid instruction.

27.charles_apot: Which poureth forth, as the Nile, instruction, and as Gihon in the days of vintage.
27.nets: It shines forth education like light, like Geon in days of vintage.
27.nrsv: It pours forth instruction like the Nile, like the Gihon at the time of vintage.
27.verse_commentary: Nile/Gihon imagery widens the river network and keeps the emphasis on abundance that reaches beyond local boundaries.

28.charles_apot: The first man knew her not perfectly, so also the last will not trace her out;
28.nets: The first man did not complete knowing her, and so the last one did not track her out.
28.nrsv: The first man did not know wisdom fully, nor will the last one fathom her.
28.verse_commentary: Human inability to exhaust Wisdom becomes a deliberate theme, not an admission of textual obscurity.

29.charles_apot: For her understanding is more full than the sea, and her counsel is greater than the deep.
29.nets: Her thought was filled from the sea, and her counsel from the great abyss.
29.nrsv: For her thoughts are more abundant than the sea, and her counsel deeper than the great abyss.
29.verse_commentary: Sea/abyss language circles back to the chapter’s cosmic opening, now in epistemic terms.

30.charles_apot: And as for me, I (was) as a stream from the river, and I came forth as a conduit into a garden;
30.nets: I, like a canal from a river and a water channel, issued forth into an orchard.
30.nrsv: As for me, I was like a canal from a river, like a water channel into a garden.
30.verse_commentary: Most commentators see a shift toward the sage’s own role here: Ben Sira channels Wisdom onward rather than generating it himself.

31.charles_apot: I said: “I will water my garden, I will abundantly water my garden beds”;
31.nets: I said, “I will water my garden, and I will drench my flower bed.”
31.nrsv: I said, ‘I will water my garden and drench my flower-beds.’ And lo, my canal became a river, and my river a sea.
31.verse_commentary: The garden image turns teaching into irrigation—cultivation, growth, and deliberate distribution.

32.charles_apot: And lo, my stream became a river, and my river became a sea.
32.nets: The canal turned into a river for me, and my river turned into a sea.
32.nrsv: I will again make instruction shine forth like the dawn, and I will make it clear from far away.
32.verse_commentary: The canal-to-river-to-sea escalation dramatizes the widening reach of instruction.

33.charles_apot: Yet again will I bring instruction to light as the morning, and will make these things shine forth afar off.
33.nets: Still I will again pour out teaching like prophecy, and I will leave it behind for generations of eternity.
33.nrsv: I will again pour out teaching like prophecy, and leave it to all future generations.
33.verse_commentary: Instruction becomes dawn/proclamation language, almost prophetic in scope.

34.charles_apot: Yet again will I pour forth doctrine as prophecy, and leave it for eternal generations. Look ye (and see), that I have not laboured for myself only, but for all those that diligently seek her.
34.nets: See that I have not toiled for myself alone but for all who seek it out.
34.nrsv: Observe that I have not laboured for myself alone, but for all who seek wisdom.
34.verse_commentary: The closing claim frames the whole chapter as public labor for seekers of wisdom, not private mysticism.
